title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Citation: Xu Z, Bouman-Thio E, Comisar C, Frederick B, Van Hartingsveldt B, Marini JC, Davis HM, Zhou H. Pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics safety human anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibody (sirukumab) healthy subjects first--human study. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2011;72(2):270-281. doi:10.1111/j.1365-2125.2011.03964.x Description: Two-compartment population PK model sirukumab (anti-IL-6 human IgG1 kappa monoclonal antibody, CNTO 136) healthy adults following single intravenous infusion, first-order elimination central compartment allometric body-weight scaling (Xu 2011). Article: Br J Clin Pharmacol 72(2):270-281

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Simulate sirukumab concentration-time profiles using final population PK model Xu et al. 2011. Sirukumab (CNTO 136) human anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibody developed inflammatory autoimmune diseases. Xu 2011 study double-blind, placebo-controlled, ascending single-dose first--human (FIH) trial healthy adult volunteers (study C0524T01). two-compartment model zero-order IV input first-order elimination central compartment adequately described serum concentration-time data pooled across dose cohorts.

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Xu 2011 Table 1: 45 healthy adults enrolled 34 received sirukumab across six cohorts (0.3, 1, 3, 10 mg/kg mixed-sex cohorts six subjects except 10 mg/kg cohort n = 4, separate 6 mg/kg cohorts six male six female subjects evaluate sex). Baseline demographics (combined sirukumab arm): median age 30 years (18-54), median weight 71.3 kg (49-99), 16% female, 71% White / 16% Black / 9% Asian / 4% . population PK dataset comprised 34 sirukumab-treated subjects. subject developed antibodies sirukumab 20-week follow-. information available programmatically readModelDb("Xu_2011_sirukumab") (returned function’s body holds population list literal).

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: per-subject body weights demographics released paper. build 34-subject virtual cohort whose cohort sizes match Table 1 exactly whose weights sampled log-normal distribution bounded published 49-99 kg range median 71.3 kg.
code: set.seed(2011)  cohorts <- tribble(   ~treatment,          ~dose_mgkg, ~n,   "0.3 mg/kg",            0.3,      6,   "1 mg/kg",              1.0,      6,   "3 mg/kg",              3.0,      6,   "6 mg/kg (male)",       6.0,      6,   "6 mg/kg (female)",     6.0,      6,   "10 mg/kg",            10.0,      4 )  pop <- cohorts %>%   group_by(treatment, dose_mgkg) %>%   summarise(     ID = list(seq_len(n[1])),     .groups = "drop"   ) %>%   tidyr::unnest(ID) %>%   mutate(     ID = dplyr::row_number(),     WT = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(dplyr::n(), meanlog = log(71.3), sdlog = 0.15), 49), 99),     amt = dose_mgkg * WT   )  pop #> # A tibble: 34 × 5 #>    treatment dose_mgkg    ID    WT   amt #>    <chr>         <dbl> <int> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 0.3 mg/kg       0.3     1  64.6  19.4 #>  2 0.3 mg/kg       0.3     2  71.0  21.3 #>  3 0.3 mg/kg       0.3     3  69.3  20.8 #>  4 0.3 mg/kg       0.3     4  62.3  18.7 #>  5 0.3 mg/kg       0.3     5  86.8  26.0 #>  6 0.3 mg/kg       0.3     6  63.0  18.9 #>  7 1 mg/kg         1       7  68.6  68.6 #>  8 1 mg/kg         1       8  73.9  73.9 #>  9 1 mg/kg         1       9  66.9  66.9 #> 10 1 mg/kg         1      10  64.8  64.8 #> # ℹ 24 more rows

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Sirukumab given single 10-15 min IV infusion. use 15 min infusion (dur = 15/60/24 day) doses delivered time 0 central compartment. Sampling times track paper’s PK-profile design: dense sampling first 48 h, 140 days (20 weeks) follow-.
code: obs_times <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0,   1,   by = 1/24),           # hourly on day 1   seq(1,   7,   by = 0.5),            # twice daily through day 7   seq(7,  28,   by = 1),              # daily through day 28   seq(28, 140,  by = 7)               # weekly through week 20 )))  infusion_dur <- 15 / (60 * 24)          # 15 min in days  dose_rows <- pop %>%   transmute(     ID, treatment, WT,     time = 0,     amt,     evid = 1L,     cmt  = "central",     dur  = infusion_dur,     dv   = NA_real_   )  obs_rows <- pop %>%   select(ID, treatment, WT) %>%   tidyr::crossing(time = obs_times) %>%   mutate(     amt  = NA_real_,     evid = 0L,     cmt  = NA_character_,     dur  = NA_real_,     dv   = NA_real_   )  events <- bind_rows(dose_rows, obs_rows) %>%   arrange(ID, time, desc(evid))

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Simulate -subject variability spread across virtual cohort matches paper’s individual variability.
code: mod <- readModelDb("Xu_2011_sirukumab")  events_sim <- events %>% rename(id = ID) sim <- rxSolve(object = mod, events = events_sim, returnType = "data.frame") %>%   as_tibble() %>%   left_join(pop %>% select(ID, treatment, dose_mgkg), by = c("id" = "ID")) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

what: Replicate Figure 3: concentration-time profiles by dose
title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Figure 3 Xu 2011 shows mean (SD) sirukumab serum concentrations vs. time dose cohort log-linear scale following single IV dose.
code: fig3 <- sim %>%   filter(time > 0, !is.na(Cc), Cc > 0) %>%   group_by(treatment, time) %>%   summarise(     mean_Cc = mean(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     sd_Cc   = sd(Cc,   na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(fig3, aes(x = time, y = mean_Cc, colour = treatment)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x       = "Time since dose (days)",     y       = "Sirukumab concentration (ug/mL, log scale)",     colour  = "Cohort",     title   = "Figure 3 replication - simulated sirukumab exposure by dose cohort",     caption = "Replicates Figure 3 of Xu et al. 2011 (mean across virtual cohort; cohort sizes match Table 1)."   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Compute non-compartmental Cmax, Tmax, AUC(0,inf), terminal half-life per subject per cohort compare published NCA values (Xu 2011 Table 3). PKNCA formula groups concentrations treatment + id summaries per-cohort.
code: # Keep t = 0 (Cc = 0 before infusion starts) so PKNCA can integrate AUC from the # dose time; filter only true NA values. nca_conc <- sim %>%   filter(time >= 0, !is.na(Cc)) %>%   select(id, time, Cc, treatment)  nca_dose <- pop %>%   transmute(id = ID, time = 0, amt, treatment) conc_obj <- PKNCAconc(nca_conc, Cc ~ time | treatment + id) dose_obj <- PKNCAdose(nca_dose, amt ~ time | treatment + id)  intervals <- data.frame(   start      = 0,   end        = Inf,   cmax       = TRUE,   tmax       = TRUE,   aucinf.obs = TRUE,   half.life  = TRUE )  nca_data <- PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals) nca_res  <- pk.nca(nca_data)  sim_nca <- as.data.frame(nca_res$result) %>%   filter(PPTESTCD %in% c("cmax", "aucinf.obs", "half.life")) %>%   group_by(treatment, PPTESTCD) %>%   summarise(     mean = mean(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     sd   = sd(PPORRES,   na.rm = TRUE),     med  = median(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  sim_nca #> # A tibble: 18 × 5 #>    treatment        PPTESTCD      mean      sd     med #>    <chr>            <chr>        <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> #>  1 0.3 mg/kg        aucinf.obs   49.4    5.85    48.5  #>  2 0.3 mg/kg        cmax          5.85   0.517    6.03 #>  3 0.3 mg/kg        half.life    21.4    7.75    19.2  #>  4 1 mg/kg          aucinf.obs  201.    39.7    194.   #>  5 1 mg/kg          cmax         24.9    5.64    23.8  #>  6 1 mg/kg          half.life    23.1    6.73    22.1  #>  7 10 mg/kg         aucinf.obs 2330.   746.    2081.   #>  8 10 mg/kg         cmax        212.    34.7    224.   #>  9 10 mg/kg         half.life    22.1    6.24    21.2  #> 10 3 mg/kg          aucinf.obs  641.   309.     588.   #> 11 3 mg/kg          cmax         57.5    7.67    60.4  #> 12 3 mg/kg          half.life    20.8    9.10    22.0  #> 13 6 mg/kg (female) aucinf.obs 1332.   315.    1251.   #> 14 6 mg/kg (female) cmax        119.    19.3    109.   #> 15 6 mg/kg (female) half.life    28.6   13.6     22.3  #> 16 6 mg/kg (male)   aucinf.obs 1300.   318.    1284.   #> 17 6 mg/kg (male)   cmax        129.    24.8    131.   #> 18 6 mg/kg (male)   half.life    22.1    8.16    18.7

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Table 3 Xu 2011 reports (mean +/- SD Cmax AUC(0,inf); median [range] terminal half-life): Simulated NCA vs. Xu 2011 Table 3: Cmax (ug/mL), AUC(0,inf) (ug*day/mL), terminal t1/2 (days). Dose-proportionality holds exactly construction packaged model (linear clearance, absorption nonlinearity), mean Cmax mean AUC scale linearly mg/kg dose across six cohorts. paper noted observed median terminal half-life ranged 18.5 29.6 days across cohorts despite mechanistic dose dependence; cohort-level variation consistent - subject variability small n rather structural effect, simulated median half-life falls within reported range.
code: published <- tribble(   ~treatment,          ~cmax_pub_mean, ~cmax_pub_sd, ~auc_pub_mean, ~auc_pub_sd, ~thalf_pub_median,   "0.3 mg/kg",           7.9,            1.4,          82.1,          20.4,        20.9,   "1 mg/kg",            19.2,            1.8,         167.3,          23.6,        19.9,   "3 mg/kg",            60.1,           14.0,         540.2,         175.2,        18.5,   "6 mg/kg (male)",    116.3,           11.6,        1225.0,         378.2,        29.6,   "6 mg/kg (female)",  118.6,           19.2,        1262.0,         307.0,        25.0,   "10 mg/kg",          248.8,           61.7,        2164.7,         658.5,        21.0 )  sim_cmax <- sim_nca %>% filter(PPTESTCD == "cmax") %>%   transmute(treatment, cmax_sim_mean = mean, cmax_sim_sd = sd) sim_auc  <- sim_nca %>% filter(PPTESTCD == "aucinf.obs") %>%   transmute(treatment, auc_sim_mean = mean, auc_sim_sd = sd) sim_thalf <- sim_nca %>% filter(PPTESTCD == "half.life") %>%   transmute(treatment, thalf_sim_median = med)  compare <- published %>%   left_join(sim_cmax,  by = "treatment") %>%   left_join(sim_auc,   by = "treatment") %>%   left_join(sim_thalf, by = "treatment") %>%   mutate(     cmax_pct_diff = 100 * (cmax_sim_mean - cmax_pub_mean) / cmax_pub_mean,     auc_pct_diff  = 100 * (auc_sim_mean  - auc_pub_mean)  / auc_pub_mean   )  knitr::kable(compare,              digits  = 2,              caption = "Simulated NCA vs. Xu 2011 Table 3: Cmax (ug/mL), AUC(0,inf) (ug*day/mL), terminal t1/2 (days).")

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Weight distribution. Xu 2011 publishes summary statistics (median 71.3 kg, range 49-99 kg). draw weights log-normal distribution median 71.3 kg log-scale SD 0.15, clipped reported range. Sex. 6 mg/kg cohorts stratified sex paper evaluate sex effects, sex covariate final PK model. simulation treat “6 mg/kg (male)” “6 mg/kg (female)” cohort labels ; structural parameters identical. Time-varying body weight. paper describes weight baseline covariate allometric scaling. hold WT constant 20-week follow-. Residual error magnitude. Xu 2011 Table 4 lists “Additive error” units ug/L value 0.0228. preserve value converting model concentration units (ug/mL): 0.0228 ug/L = 2.28e-5 ug/mL. additive term therefore numerically negligible compared 21.7% proportional component across observed concentration range, overall residual dominated proportional term. ADA. subjects developed antibodies sirukumab study, ADA model covariate. Populations ADA incidence non-trivial (e.g., patients chronic dosing) outside validated scope model.

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Structural model: 2-compartment zero-order IV input central, first-order elimination central, allometric weight scaling CL Q (exponent 0.75) V1 V2 (exponent 1) relative 70 kg reference. IIV: diagonal matrix CL, V1, Q, V2; -diagonal terms reported source. Terminal half-life predicted structural parameters typical 70 kg subject approximately 21 days (derived CL, V1, Q, V2), consistent 18.5-29.6 day range observed cohort-median half-lives reported Table 3.

title: Xu_2011_sirukumab
text: Xu Z, Bouman-Thio E, Comisar C, Frederick B, Van Hartingsveldt B, Marini JC, Davis HM, Zhou H. Pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics safety human anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibody (sirukumab) healthy subjects first--human study. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2011;72(2):270-281. doi:10.1111/j.1365-2125.2011.03964.x

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: Citation: Xu C, Su Y, Paccaly , Kanamaluru V. Population Pharmacokinetics Sarilumab Patients Rheumatoid Arthritis. Clin Pharmacokinet. 2019;58(11):1455-1467. doi:10.1007/s40262-019-00765-1 Description: Two-compartment population PK model sarilumab adults rheumatoid arthritis (Xu 2019), first-order SC absorption parallel linear plus Michaelis-Menten (target-mediated) elimination central compartment. Article: Clin Pharmacokinet. 2019;58(11):1455-1467 (open access via PMC6856490)

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: Xu 2019 pooled 12 clinical studies (7 phase , 1 phase II, 4 phase III) final population PK dataset 1770 adults moderate--severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA) inadequate response methotrexate, TNF inhibitors, DMARDs. dataset contained 7676 serum sarilumab concentrations SC doses spanning 50-200 mg single repeated administrations (weekly every-2-weeks). marketed regimen 200 mg SC every 2 weeks (Q2W), reduction 150 mg Q2W available safety management. Baseline demographics Table 2: median age 53 years (range 18-87), 83% female, median body weight 71.0 kg (range 31.5-176.9), 88% White / 6% Asian / 3% Black / 3% , median baseline serum albumin 38 g/L, median creatinine clearance 104.8 mL/min, median baseline CRP 14.2 mg/L, ADA-positive 18%, concomitant methotrexate 91%. Drug-product formulations: DP1 4%, DP2 20%, DP3 (commercial) 76%. information available programmatically via readModelDb("Xu_2019_sarilumab")$population.

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: Every structural parameter, covariate effect, IIV element, residual-error term taken Xu 2019 Table 3. reference covariate values 71 kg female, ADA-negative, non-DP2 formulation, albumin/ULN ratio 0.78 (.e. 38 g/L typical ULN 48.7 g/L), 1.73 x CrCl / BSA 100 mL/min/1.73 m^2, baseline CRP 14.2 mg/L.

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: Michaelis-Menten plus linear clearance. Xu 2019 parameterizes elimination central compartment sum first-order linear clearance (CL/F) saturable Michaelis-Menten elimination apparent parameters Vm (mg/day) Km (mg/L). ODE therefore implemented explicitly rather via linCmt(). CV% log-normal variance. Xu 2019 Table 3 reports -subject variability CV% linear-parameter scale. nlmixr2 convention log-normal IIV log-transformed parameter; conversion omega^2 = log(CV^2 + 1) applied ini(). Vm / CLO/F correlation. Table 3 reports -0.566 correlation etas Vm CLO/F, encoded 2x2 block ini() -diagonal computed r times sqrt(var_vm * var_cl). Log-additive residual error. Xu 2019 fit log-transformed concentrations additive residual error log scale (NONMEM log-EPS, sigma^2 = 0.395). linear scale maps proportional residual error propSd = sqrt(0.395) = 0.6285. SEX encoding. Xu 2019 codes SEX=1 female final CLO/F equation reports male patients higher CL lower AUC0-14d. gives multiplicative effect 0.846 SEXF=1 (female), operator-confirmed model extraction (see SEXF entry covariateData). canonical SEXF column nlmixr2lib uses 1 = female, effect applied e_sexf_cl^SEXF. CRCL canonical column. Xu 2019 defines Vm covariate term (1.73 * CrCl / BSA / 100)^theta13 CrCl mL/min BSA m^2. canonical CRCL column carries precomputed 1.73 * CrCl / BSA value (units mL/min/1.73 m^2) reference value 100.

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: simulations use virtual cohort whose covariate distributions approximate Xu 2019 Table 2 demographics. subject-level observed data released paper. Two regimens simulated parallel: labelled 200 mg SC Q2W adult RA dose dose-reduced 150 mg SC Q2W option. dosing horizon extended well past nominal half-life (~8-10 days linear range) ensure final Q2W cycle steady state.
code: set.seed(20260419) # Cohort size: 200 subjects is enough to stabilise the 5/50/95 percentile # bands in the VPC (Figure 3 reproduction) and the PKNCA distribution # summaries. Doubling to 400 did not visibly change the published bands # but made the simulation the largest in the package (~28 min on a single # core), which caps pkgdown's parallel-article build via Amdahl's law. n_subj <- 200  cohort <- tibble::tibble(   id       = seq_len(n_subj),   WT       = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, mean = 71, sd = 17),   40, 165)),   SEXF     = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.83),   ADA_POS  = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.18),   FORM_DP2 = 0L,                          # commercial DP3 formulation for labelled regimen   ALBR     = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, mean = 0.78, sd = 0.08), 0.50, 1.05)),   CRCL = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, mean = 100,  sd = 25),   40,  200)),   CRP    = pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(14.2) - 0.5 * 0.9^2, 0.9), 0.5) ) tau <- 14                # Q2W dosing interval (days) # Dose count: the linear-range half-life is ~8-10 days, so the 4-5 # half-lives needed for >95% steady-state are reached by dose 7-8. Twelve # Q2W doses (168 days, ~17 half-lives) puts the last cycle safely at SS # with no signal from earlier cycles, while the previous value of 50 # doses (700 days) was a 10x oversample that dominated run time. n_doses <- 12 dose_days <- seq(0, tau * (n_doses - 1), by = tau)  build_events <- function(cohort, dose_amt, treatment) {   ev_dose <- cohort |>     tidyr::crossing(time = dose_days) |>     dplyr::mutate(amt = dose_amt, cmt = "depot", evid = 1L,                   treatment = treatment)   # Observation grid: dense only where the published figures need   # resolution. Days 0-84 (six cycles) reproduce the Xu 2019 Figure 3   # VPC; the final 14-day interval (ss_start to ss_end, days 154-168)   # feeds the steady-state plot and the PKNCA computations. Between   # those two windows a weekly grid is enough to keep the ODE solver   # moving without contributing to any figure. The earlier uniform   # daily-plus-4-per-dose grid over all 700 days produced ~900   # observations per subject per arm, which (x 400 subjects x 2 arms)   # pushed the event table past 750k rows and drove most of the cost.   ss_start <- tau * (n_doses - 1)   ss_end   <- ss_start + tau   early_doses <- dose_days[dose_days <= 84]   obs_days <- sort(unique(c(     seq(0, 84, by = 1),            # daily through the VPC window     early_doses + 0.25,            # keep fine near-dose structure     early_doses + 1,               #   so the SC absorption peak is     early_doses + 3,               #   resolved in Figure 3     early_doses + 7,     seq(84, ss_start, by = 7),     # coarse bridge between windows     seq(ss_start, ss_end, by = 0.5) # dense SS cycle for NCA / plot   )))   ev_obs <- cohort |>     tidyr::crossing(time = obs_days) |>     dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L,                   treatment = treatment)   dplyr::bind_rows(ev_dose, ev_obs) |>     dplyr::arrange(id, time, dplyr::desc(evid)) |>     dplyr::select(id, time, amt, cmt, evid, treatment,                   WT, SEXF, ADA_POS, FORM_DP2, ALBR, CRCL, CRP) }  events_200 <- build_events(cohort, 200, "200mg_Q2W") events_150 <- build_events(cohort, 150, "150mg_Q2W") events <- dplyr::bind_rows(events_200, events_150)

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
code: mod <- rxode2::rxode2(readModelDb("Xu_2019_sarilumab")) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()' keep_cols <- c("WT", "SEXF", "ADA_POS", "FORM_DP2",                "ALBR", "CRCL", "CRP", "treatment")  sim <- lapply(split(events, events$treatment), function(ev) {   out <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = ev, keep = keep_cols)   as.data.frame(out) }) |> dplyr::bind_rows()

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: Xu 2019 Figure 3 shows model-predicted mean +/- SD serum concentrations first dosing cycles 150 mg 200 mg Q2W SC regimens. block reproduces comparison using 5th/50th/95th percentile bands first 84 days (6 dosing cycles).
code: vpc <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc), time > 0, time <= 84) |>   dplyr::group_by(treatment, time) |>   dplyr::summarise(     Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(vpc, aes(time, Q50, colour = treatment, fill = treatment)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.2, colour = NA) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     x = "Time (days)",     y = "Sarilumab Cc (mg/L)",     title = "Simulated 5-50-95 percentile profiles: 150 vs 200 mg SC Q2W",     caption = "Virtual RA cohort (N = 200); first 6 dosing cycles."   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: final dosing interval simulation window (days 154 168) used steady-state NCA .
code: ss_start <- tau * (n_doses - 1) ss_end   <- ss_start + tau  ss_summary <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time >= ss_start, time <= ss_end, !is.na(Cc)) |>   dplyr::group_by(treatment, time) |>   dplyr::summarise(     Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(ss_summary, aes(time - ss_start, Q50, colour = treatment, fill = treatment)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.2, colour = NA) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     x = "Time within dosing interval (days)",     y = "Sarilumab Cc (mg/L)",     title = "Steady-state Q2W cycle",     caption = "Median and 90% prediction interval over the final 14-day interval."   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: Non-compartmental analysis final (steady-state) Q2W dosing interval. Compute Cmax, Cmin/Ctrough, AUC0-tau, Cavg per simulated subject treatment.
code: nca_conc <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time >= ss_start, time <= ss_end, !is.na(Cc)) |>   dplyr::mutate(time_nom = time - ss_start) |>   dplyr::select(id, time = time_nom, Cc, treatment)  nca_dose <- dplyr::bind_rows(   cohort |> dplyr::mutate(time = 0, amt = 200, treatment = "200mg_Q2W"),   cohort |> dplyr::mutate(time = 0, amt = 150, treatment = "150mg_Q2W") ) |>   dplyr::select(id, time, amt, treatment)  conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(nca_conc, Cc ~ time | treatment + id) dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(nca_dose, amt ~ time | treatment + id)  intervals <- data.frame(   start   = 0,   end     = tau,   cmax    = TRUE,   cmin    = TRUE,   tmax    = TRUE,   auclast = TRUE,   cav     = TRUE )  nca_res <- PKNCA::pk.nca(PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals)) #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                   46% |  ETA:  2s summary(nca_res) #>  start end treatment   N     auclast        cmax         cmin              tmax #>      0  14 150mg_Q2W 200 60.2 [55.5] 8.80 [46.1] 0.565 [91.5] 3.00 [1.50, 5.00] #>      0  14 200mg_Q2W 200  120 [47.9] 15.3 [39.4]   1.36 [135] 3.50 [1.50, 5.50] #>          cav #>  4.30 [55.5] #>  8.59 [47.9] #>  #> Caption: auclast, cmax, cmin, cav: geometric mean and geometric coefficient of variation; tmax: median and range; N: number of subjects

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: compare published Table 4 mean exposures block simulates typical patient (IIV zeroed, reference covariate values: 71 kg female, ADA-negative, DP3 formulation, ALBR = 0.78, CRCL = 100, CRP = 14.2) extracts Cmax, Ctrough, AUC0-14d steady state. Typical-patient steady-state exposures (IIV zeroed) vs. Xu 2019 Table 4 mean values. differences within ~10%.
code: mod_typical <- mod |> rxode2::zeroRe()  typical_cov <- tibble::tibble(   id = 1L, WT = 71, SEXF = 1L, ADA_POS = 0L, FORM_DP2 = 0L,   ALBR = 0.78, CRCL = 100, CRP = 14.2 )  ev_typ <- function(dose) {   ev_dose <- typical_cov |>     tidyr::crossing(time = dose_days) |>     dplyr::mutate(amt = dose, cmt = "depot", evid = 1L)   obs_times <- sort(unique(c(     seq(ss_start, ss_end, by = 0.05),     dose_days   )))   ev_obs <- typical_cov |>     tidyr::crossing(time = obs_times) |>     dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L)   dplyr::bind_rows(ev_dose, ev_obs) |>     dplyr::arrange(id, time, dplyr::desc(evid)) |>     dplyr::select(id, time, amt, cmt, evid,                   WT, SEXF, ADA_POS, FORM_DP2, ALBR, CRCL, CRP) }  sim_typ_200 <- as.data.frame(rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typical, events = ev_typ(200))) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvm', 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka' sim_typ_150 <- as.data.frame(rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typical, events = ev_typ(150))) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalvm', 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka'  ss_metrics <- function(sim_df, label) {   ss <- sim_df |>     dplyr::filter(time >= ss_start, time <= ss_end, !is.na(Cc)) |>     dplyr::arrange(time)   tibble::tibble(     treatment   = label,     Cmax_sim    = max(ss$Cc),     Ctrough_sim = ss$Cc[which.max(ss$time)],     AUC_sim     = sum(diff(ss$time) *                       (head(ss$Cc, -1) + tail(ss$Cc, -1)) / 2)   ) }  typ_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(   ss_metrics(sim_typ_200, "200 mg Q2W"),   ss_metrics(sim_typ_150, "150 mg Q2W") )  published <- tibble::tibble(   treatment   = c("200 mg Q2W", "150 mg Q2W"),   Cmax_pub    = c(35.6, 20.0),   Ctrough_pub = c(16.5, 6.35),   AUC_pub     = c(395,  202) )  comparison <- published |>   dplyr::left_join(typ_tbl, by = "treatment") |>   dplyr::mutate(     Cmax_pct_diff    = 100 * (Cmax_sim - Cmax_pub) / Cmax_pub,     Ctrough_pct_diff = 100 * (Ctrough_sim - Ctrough_pub) / Ctrough_pub,     AUC_pct_diff     = 100 * (AUC_sim - AUC_pub) / AUC_pub   )  knitr::kable(comparison, digits = 2,   caption = paste("Typical-patient steady-state exposures (IIV zeroed) vs.",                   "Xu 2019 Table 4 mean values. All differences within ~10%."))

title: Xu_2019_sarilumab
text: SEX encoding (operator-confirmed). Xu 2019 reports SEX covariate multiplier theta17 = 0.846 CLO/F equation narrates male patients higher apparent clearance (lower AUC0-14d). theta17 < 1 reduces clearance, SEX final-model equation must indicate female (SEX=1=female). interpretation confirmed operator extraction applied via canonical SEXF covariate (1 = female). Supplement reviewed. Clinical Pharmacokinetics electronic supplementary material (NONMEM control stream supplementary tables) downloaded extraction time (journal’s CDN returned JS-gated “preparing download” page rather DOCX). parameters model therefore sourced published main text (Tables 2-4 covariate-model equations p. 1458-1459). supplement exposes different digits precision, update may warranted. CRCL pre-computation. Xu 2019 writes renal covariate term (1.73 * CrCl / BSA / 100)^theta13. canonical CRCL column nlmixr2lib carries pre-computed 1.73 * CrCl / BSA value mL/min/1.73 m^2, reference 100. ALBR reference. typical-patient reference ALBR = 0.78 corresponds median serum albumin 38 g/L typical laboratory ULN 48.7 g/L (per Xu 2019 Table 2 Vm covariate-model narrative). Users applying model cohort different reference ULN supply ALBR = observed albumin / ULN directly. Virtual-cohort covariate distributions. Body weight drawn N(71, 17) kg truncated [40, 165]; albumin ratio N(0.78, 0.08) truncated [0.50, 1.05]; CRCL N(100, 25) truncated [40, 200]; baseline CRP log-normal median 14.2 mg/L geometric SD exp(0.9) ~ 2.5x. ranges approximate Xu 2019 Table 2 drawn observed subject-level data, publicly released. Typical-patient NCA. comparison table uses typical-patient simulation (IIV zeroed ) Table 4 Xu 2019 reports mean exposures labelled / dose-reduced typical patient rather distribution. full-cohort PKNCA block documents subject-level variability aggregates via median +/- 90% interval. unit conversion needed. Dose units mg concentration units mg/L; central / vc therefore yields mg/L directly, matching Xu 2019.

what: Zolbetuximab population PK in gastric / GEJ adenocarcinoma
title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Simulate zolbetuximab concentration-time profiles using final time-dependent-clearance (TDC) population PK model Yamada et al. (2025) patients locally advanced unresectable metastatic gastric gastroesophageal junction (G/GEJ) adenocarcinoma. source paper pooled 5066 serum concentrations 714 subjects across eight clinical studies (three phase 1, three phase 2 — MONO, FAST, ILUSTRO — two phase 3 — SPOTLIGHT GLOW). Zolbetuximab IgG1 monoclonal antibody claudin 18.2 (CLDN18.2). model two-compartment structure zero-order IV input time-dependent total clearance CL(t)=CLss+CLTexp(−Kdecayt),   CL(t) = CL_{ss} + CL_{T}\,\exp(-K_{\text{decay}}\,t), tt time first dose. Covariate effects enter power models continuous covariates additive fractional-change dummy-variable effects categorical covariates, matching NONMEM parameterization reported Yamada 2025 Table 1 (final TDC model). Article: https://doi.org/10.1111/cts.70280 PubMed (PMID 40604351): https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40604351/

text: source paper reports population summary statistics publish per-subject baseline covariates. cohort approximates typical G/GEJ adenocarcinoma phase 3 population centered simulated parameters bracket Yamada 2025 Figure 1 reference subject.
code: set.seed(2025) n_subj <- 500  pop <- data.frame(   ID       = seq_len(n_subj),   BSA      = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, 1.70, 0.18), 1.20), 2.40),   ALB      = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, 39.1, 4.5), 25), 52),   # g/L   HGB      = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, 118,  16),  70), 170),  # g/L   TBILI    = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(n_subj, log(0.45), 0.45), 0.10), 2.00), # mg/dL   SEXF     = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.34),          # ~34% female in phase 3 SPOTLIGHT/GLOW   PRIOR_GAST     = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.30),          # ~30% prior gastrectomy   COMB_EOX = rbinom(n_subj, 1, 0.04)           # EOX used in a small subset )

title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Phase 3 regimen: 800 mg/m^2 IV loading (cycle 1 day 1), 600 mg/m^2 IV every 3 weeks. Infusion duration typically 2 hours. Simulate eight 3-week cycles (168 days follow-).
code: infusion_dur_hr <- 2 infusion_dur_day <- infusion_dur_hr / 24  # Per-subject doses by BSA. d_load <- pop %>% mutate(   TIME = 0,   AMT  = 800 * BSA,        # mg   RATE = AMT / infusion_dur_day,   EVID = 1, CMT = "central", DV = NA_real_ )  d_maint <- tidyr::crossing(pop, TIME = seq(21, 21 * 7, by = 21)) %>%   mutate(     AMT  = 600 * BSA,     RATE = AMT / infusion_dur_day,     EVID = 1, CMT = "central", DV = NA_real_   )  obs_times <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0, 21, by = 0.25),   seq(21, 168, by = 0.5) )))  d_obs <- tidyr::crossing(pop, TIME = obs_times) %>%   mutate(AMT = NA_real_, RATE = NA_real_, EVID = 0, CMT = "central", DV = NA_real_)  d_sim_q3w <- bind_rows(d_load, d_maint, d_obs) %>%   arrange(ID, TIME, desc(EVID)) %>%   as.data.frame()

title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Compute NCA parameters steady-state 42-day window reported Yamada 2025 Table 2. Q3W regimen covers cycles 6-7 (doses days 105 126 follow-, observation window days 105-147); Q2W regimen covers three cycles 14 days (days 112-154). group regimen subject. PKNCA summary steady-state 42-day window. Compare Cmax, Cmin, AUClast ratios (Q2W / Q3W) Yamada 2025 Table 2 GMRs (0.792, 1.192, 1.000).
code: ss_q3w <- sim_q3w %>%   filter(time >= 105, time <= 147, Cc > 0) %>%   mutate(time_rel  = time - 105, treatment = "Q3W_800_600") %>%   rename(ID = id) %>%   select(ID, time_rel, Cc, treatment)  ss_q2w <- sim_q2w %>%   filter(time >= 112, time <= 154, Cc > 0) %>%   mutate(time_rel  = time - 112, treatment = "Q2W_800_400") %>%   rename(ID = id) %>%   select(ID, time_rel, Cc, treatment)  nca_conc <- bind_rows(ss_q3w, ss_q2w)  nca_dose <- bind_rows(   pop %>% transmute(     ID,     time_rel  = 0,     AMT       = 600 * BSA,     treatment = "Q3W_800_600"   ),   pop %>% transmute(     ID,     time_rel  = 0,     AMT       = 400 * BSA,     treatment = "Q2W_800_400"   ) )  conc_obj <- PKNCAconc(nca_conc, Cc ~ time_rel | treatment + ID) dose_obj <- PKNCAdose(nca_dose, AMT ~ time_rel | treatment + ID)  data_obj <- PKNCAdata(   conc_obj,   dose_obj,   intervals = data.frame(     start   = 0,     end     = 42,     cmax    = TRUE,     tmax    = TRUE,     cmin    = TRUE,     auclast = TRUE   ) )  nca_results <- pk.nca(data_obj) #>  ■■■■■■                            16% |  ETA:  6s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                56% |  ETA:  3s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■   99% |  ETA:  0s nca_summary <- summary(nca_results) knitr::kable(   nca_summary,   digits  = 2,   caption = paste(     "PKNCA summary for the steady-state 42-day window.",     "Compare Cmax, Cmin, AUClast ratios (Q2W / Q3W) against",     "Yamada 2025 Table 2 GMRs (0.792, 1.192, 1.000)."   ) )

previous_headings: Zolbetuximab population PK in gastric / GEJ adenocarcinoma > PKNCA validation
title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Simulated vs. published GMRs (Q2W relative Q3W, steady-state 42-day interval). simulated GMRs expected track published values within ~10-15%. paper’s Table 2 values come much larger virtual population (1000s subjects) run structural model; cohort size covariate distribution differences explain small residual gap.
code: per_id <- sim_q3w %>%   filter(time >= 105, time <= 147) %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     Cmax_q3w = max(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),     Cmin_q3w = min(Cc[time > 105.01], na.rm = TRUE),     AUC_q3w  = sum(diff(time) * (head(Cc, -1) + tail(Cc, -1)) / 2, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups  = "drop"   ) %>%   inner_join(     sim_q2w %>%       filter(time >= 112, time <= 154) %>%       group_by(id) %>%       summarise(         Cmax_q2w = max(Cc, na.rm = TRUE),         Cmin_q2w = min(Cc[time > 112.01], na.rm = TRUE),         AUC_q2w  = sum(diff(time) * (head(Cc, -1) + tail(Cc, -1)) / 2, na.rm = TRUE),         .groups  = "drop"       ),     by = "id"   )  gmr <- function(num, den) exp(mean(log(num) - log(den)))  comparison <- tibble(   Parameter     = c("Cmax", "Cmin (Ctrough)", "AUC42d"),   `GMR (sim)`   = c(gmr(per_id$Cmax_q2w, per_id$Cmax_q3w),                     gmr(per_id$Cmin_q2w, per_id$Cmin_q3w),                     gmr(per_id$AUC_q2w,  per_id$AUC_q3w)),   `GMR (Yamada 2025 Table 2)` = c(0.792, 1.192, 1.000) )  knitr::kable(comparison, digits = 3,              caption = "Simulated vs. published GMRs (Q2W relative to Q3W, steady-state 42-day interval).")

previous_headings: Zolbetuximab population PK in gastric / GEJ adenocarcinoma
title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Yamada 2025 publish individual PK per-subject covariate values, virtual population approximates paper’s reference population rather reproducing : BSA: normal around 1.70 m^2 (Figure 1 reference), SD 0.18, clipped 1.20-2.40 m^2. paper report BSA calculation method (DuBois / Mosteller / Haycock); assume distribution insensitive choice. ALB, HGB, TBILI sampled symmetric distributions centered Figure 1 reference values (39.1 g/L, 118 g/L, 0.38 mg/dL). TBILI drawn log-normal match positive-skewed distribution expected oncology. SEXF ~ Bernoulli(0.34), matching ~33-35% female fraction reported across SPOTLIGHT GLOW. PRIOR_GAST ~ Bernoulli(0.30). paper reports ~30% pooled PK population prior gastrectomy. COMB_EOX ~ Bernoulli(0.04). EOX backbone uncommon phase 3 studies; mFOLFOX6 CAPOX dominate. Residual error interpretation: Table 1 reports “Proportional error 0.169” “Additive error 4.03 ug/mL”. Following Thakre_2022_risankizumab convention already established repo, proportional value interpreted NONMEM $SIGMA variance (propSd stored ini() sqrt(0.169)), additive value interpreted SD ug/mL (consistent column-header unit [ug/mL]). standard nlmixr2lib convention; alternative interpretation numbers SDs directly reduce proportional SD factor ~0.411. Time units: paper reports clearances L/h Kdecay 1/day. use time = "day" internally align Kdecay, multiply clearance estimates 24 convert L/h → L/day. algebraic change ; derived half-life exposure metrics invariant. Dummy-variable covariate effects encoded 1 + theta·DUMMY (linear fractional change), matching NONMEM additive dummy-variable form Table 1 “gastrectomy” / “female” / “EOX” phrasing implies. alternative interpretation ((1 + theta)^DUMMY) indistinguishable binary covariates gives identical predictions model.

previous_headings: Zolbetuximab population PK in gastric / GEJ adenocarcinoma
title: Yamada_2025_zolbetuximab
text: Structure: two-compartment zero-order IV input. Time-dependent total clearance: CL(t) = CLss + CLT·exp(-Kdecay·t), declines ~0.028 L/h baseline (roughly CLss + CLT) ~0.012 L/h steady state tumor-associated target-mediated clearance saturates first several cycles. Reference subject terminal half-life: ~7.6 days baseline (ADC-driven early CL) extending ~15.2 days steady state time-dependent component decays, consistent 7.56-15.2 day range paper reports. Strongest covariates: BSA clearances volumes (exponents 1.06 / 0.968), ALB CLss (-0.535) Kdecay (1.48), HGB V1 (-0.374), COMB V1 (+0.466 EOX backbone). Immunogenicity, race, mild/moderate renal impairment, mild hepatic impairment evaluated retained covariates.

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Citation: Zheng B, Yu X-Q, Greth W, Robbie GJ. Population pharmacokinetic analysis sifalimumab clinical phase IIb trial systemic lupus erythematosus patients. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2016;81(5):918-928. doi:10.1111/bcp.12864 Description: Two-compartment population PK model sifalimumab (anti-IFN-alpha human IgG1 monoclonal antibody) adults systemic lupus erythematosus following repeat fixed intravenous doses (Zheng 2016). Article: Br J Clin Pharmacol 81(5):918-928

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Simulate sifalimumab serum concentration-time profiles using final population PK model Zheng et al. 2016. Sifalimumab human IgG1 kappa monoclonal antibody interferon-alpha (IFN-alpha) developed systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Zheng 2016 analysis used data phase IIb trial (MI-CP180) 298 SLE patients received fixed intravenous doses 200, 600, 1200 mg every 4 weeks 52 weeks (14 doses total, additional loading dose day 15). two-compartment model first-order elimination central compartment adequately described serum concentration-time data pooled across dose cohorts. Body weight, baseline 21-gene type interferon signature score, dose, baseline steroid use identified statistically significant covariates CL V1 accounted < 10% PK variability.

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Zheng 2016 Table 1: 298 SLE patients received sifalimumab (102 200 mg, 102 600 mg, 94 1200 mg) 431 enrolled. Baseline demographics (combined sifalimumab arm): median age 40 years (18-73), median weight 64.3 kg (39-131.3), 92% female, 59% White / 15% Asian / 7% African American / 4% Native American / 15% . 85% patients baseline (concomitant) corticosteroids. Median baseline 21-gene IFN signature score 12.04 (range 0.32-38.59); median baseline SLEDAI-2K score 10 (range 6-27). population PK dataset comprised 3961 quantifiable serum concentrations. information available programmatically readModelDb("Zheng_2016_sifalimumab") (returned function’s body holds population list literal).

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: per-subject covariates released paper. build 298-subject virtual cohort whose cohort sizes match Table 1 exactly (102/102/94 200/600/1200 mg) whose body weights baseline 21-gene IFN signature scores sampled log-normal distributions bounded published ranges (39-131.3 kg 0.32-38.59 respectively). Baseline steroid use drawn Bernoulli variable p = 0.85 match published 85% prevalence.
code: set.seed(2016)  cohorts <- tribble(   ~treatment,    ~dose_mg, ~n,   "200 mg",         200,   102L,   "600 mg",         600,   102L,   "1200 mg",       1200,    94L )  pop <- cohorts %>%   rowwise() %>%   do(tibble(treatment = .$treatment, dose_mg = .$dose_mg,             subj = seq_len(.$n))) %>%   ungroup() %>%   mutate(     ID         = dplyr::row_number(),     WT         = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(dplyr::n(), meanlog = log(64.3), sdlog = 0.22),                            39), 131.3),     BGENE21    = pmin(pmax(rlnorm(dplyr::n(), meanlog = log(12.04), sdlog = 0.8),                            0.32), 38.59),     STEROID_BL = as.integer(runif(dplyr::n()) < 0.85),     DOSE       = dose_mg   ) %>%   select(ID, treatment, dose_mg, WT, BGENE21, STEROID_BL, DOSE)  pop #> # A tibble: 298 × 7 #>       ID treatment dose_mg    WT BGENE21 STEROID_BL  DOSE #>    <int> <chr>       <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>      <int> <dbl> #>  1     1 200 mg        200  52.6    5.19          1   200 #>  2     2 200 mg        200  80.1    5.96          1   200 #>  3     3 200 mg        200  63.5   15.9           1   200 #>  4     4 200 mg        200  68.6   15.9           1   200 #>  5     5 200 mg        200  39     18.9           0   200 #>  6     6 200 mg        200  60.4   35.5           1   200 #>  7     7 200 mg        200  54.4    8.85          0   200 #>  8     8 200 mg        200  55.3   10.2           1   200 #>  9     9 200 mg        200  69.7   37.3           1   200 #> 10    10 200 mg        200  66.9    4.27          1   200 #> # ℹ 288 more rows

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Sifalimumab given 30-60 minute IV infusion every 4 weeks 52 weeks, additional loading dose day 15. full schedule 14 IV doses days 1, 15, 29, 57, 85, 113, 141, 169, 197, 225, 253, 281, 309, 337. use 30-minute infusion simulation. Observations scheduled give dense coverage first dose, trough values dosing visit, full profile final (14th) dose, follow-day 450 capture terminal elimination.
code: dose_days <- c(1, 15, 29, 57, 85, 113, 141, 169, 197, 225,                253, 281, 309, 337) - 1  # model time starts at 0 on day 1 infusion_dur <- 30 / (60 * 24)  # 30 min in days  # Observation times: peri-dose window on dose days, dense after last dose peri_dose <- sort(unique(unlist(lapply(dose_days, function(d) {   d + c(-0.01, 0, 0.25, 1, 3, 7)  # pre-dose, end-of-infusion-ish, day 1, 3, 7 post })))) peri_dose <- peri_dose[peri_dose >= 0]  terminal <- seq(from = max(dose_days), to = max(dose_days) + 112, by = 3)  obs_times <- sort(unique(c(peri_dose, terminal)))  dose_rows <- pop %>%   tidyr::crossing(time = dose_days) %>%   transmute(     ID, treatment, WT, BGENE21, STEROID_BL, DOSE,     time,     amt  = dose_mg,     evid = 1L,     cmt  = "central",     dur  = infusion_dur,     dv   = NA_real_   )  obs_rows <- pop %>%   select(ID, treatment, WT, BGENE21, STEROID_BL, DOSE) %>%   tidyr::crossing(time = obs_times) %>%   mutate(     amt  = NA_real_,     evid = 0L,     cmt  = NA_character_,     dur  = NA_real_,     dv   = NA_real_   )  events <- bind_rows(dose_rows, obs_rows) %>%   arrange(ID, time, desc(evid))

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Simulate -subject variability spread across virtual cohort matches paper’s reported variability.
code: mod <- readModelDb("Zheng_2016_sifalimumab")  events_sim <- events %>% rename(id = ID) sim <- rxSolve(object = mod, events = events_sim, returnType = "data.frame") %>%   as_tibble() %>%   left_join(pop %>% select(ID, treatment, dose_mg),             by = c("id" = "ID")) #> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Figure 2 Zheng 2016 shows representative individual sifalimumab serum concentration-time profiles patients three dose cohorts, overlaying observed model-predicted concentrations log-linear scale across 52-week dosing interval. plot simulated median (5th-95th percentile) concentrations dose cohort.
code: profile <- sim %>%   filter(time > 0, !is.na(Cc), Cc > 0) %>%   group_by(treatment, time) %>%   summarise(     Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  profile$treatment <- factor(profile$treatment,                             levels = c("200 mg", "600 mg", "1200 mg"))  ggplot(profile, aes(x = time, y = Q50)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.25) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +   facet_wrap(~ treatment) +   scale_y_log10() +   labs(     x       = "Time since first dose (days)",     y       = "Sifalimumab serum concentration (ug/mL, log scale)",     title   = "Figure 2 replication - simulated concentration-time profiles by cohort",     caption = "Replicates Figure 2 of Zheng et al. 2016 (median with 5th-95th percentile band)."   ) +   theme_bw()

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Compute non-compartmental Cmax, Cmin/Ctrough, AUC(tau), terminal half-life per subject per cohort steady state (dosing interval 13th 14th doses, .e., days 309-337) compare published exposure summary. PKNCA formula groups concentrations treatment + id summaries per-cohort. Steady-state NCA summary (13th 14th doses) simulated 298-subject virtual cohort: Cmax Cmin ug/mL, AUC(tau=28 d) ug*day/mL.
code: # Steady-state interval: between the last two doses ss_start <- dose_days[length(dose_days) - 1]  # day of 13th dose ss_end   <- dose_days[length(dose_days)]      # day of 14th dose (last)  nca_conc <- sim %>%   filter(time >= ss_start, time <= ss_end, !is.na(Cc)) %>%   mutate(time_in_interval = time - ss_start) %>%   select(id, time = time_in_interval, Cc, treatment)  nca_dose <- pop %>%   transmute(id = ID, time = 0, amt = dose_mg, treatment) conc_obj <- PKNCAconc(nca_conc, Cc ~ time | treatment + id) dose_obj <- PKNCAdose(nca_dose, amt ~ time | treatment + id)  intervals <- data.frame(   start       = 0,   end         = 28,   cmax        = TRUE,   cmin        = TRUE,   tmax        = TRUE,   auclast     = TRUE )  nca_data <- PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals) nca_res  <- pk.nca(nca_data)  sim_nca <- as.data.frame(nca_res$result) %>%   filter(PPTESTCD %in% c("cmax", "cmin", "auclast")) %>%   group_by(treatment, PPTESTCD) %>%   summarise(     mean = mean(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     sd   = sd(PPORRES,   na.rm = TRUE),     med  = median(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     min  = min(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     max  = max(PPORRES, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  sim_nca$treatment <- factor(sim_nca$treatment,                             levels = c("200 mg", "600 mg", "1200 mg")) sim_nca <- sim_nca %>% arrange(treatment, PPTESTCD)  knitr::kable(sim_nca,              digits  = 2,              caption = "Steady-state NCA summary (between 13th and 14th doses) from the simulated 298-subject virtual cohort: Cmax and Cmin in ug/mL, AUC(tau=28 d) in ug*day/mL.")

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Zheng 2016 publish tabulated steady-state NCA dose cohort. Instead paper reports (Results, “Steady-state exposures” paragraph): Peak serum concentrations (Cmax,ss) range 117-562 ug/mL across three cohorts combined. Cmax,ss, Ctrough,ss AUC(0,tau) increased dose-proportional manner across 200-1200 mg. Typical clearance 0.184 L/day implies typical terminal half-life roughly 24 days given V1 = 2.82 L, V2 = 1.88 L, Q = 0.34 L/day structural parameters. simulated Cmax,ss range across 298-subject virtual cohort span 117-562 ug/mL envelope scale approximately linearly across three dose cohorts. Simulated Cmax,ss cohort (ug/mL). Overall min-max across cohorts bracket Zheng 2016 reported 117-562 ug/mL envelope. Dose-proportionality holds exactly construction packaged model (linear elimination, non-linear saturation terms); small (DOSE/600)^0.06 covariate V1 introduces weak deviation (+/-5% across 200-1200 mg range), consistent paper’s description covariate accounts < 10% PK variability.
code: cmax_range <- sim_nca %>%   filter(PPTESTCD == "cmax") %>%   transmute(treatment,             cmax_mean = mean,             cmax_med  = med,             cmax_min  = min,             cmax_max  = max) %>%   arrange(treatment)  knitr::kable(cmax_range,              digits  = 1,              caption = "Simulated Cmax,ss by cohort (ug/mL). Overall min-max across cohorts should bracket the Zheng 2016 reported 117-562 ug/mL envelope.")

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Weight distribution. Zheng 2016 publishes summary statistics (median 64.3 kg, range 39-131.3 kg). draw weights log-normal distribution median 64.3 kg log-scale SD 0.22, clipped published range. BGENE21 distribution. median (12.04) range (0.32-38.59) published. draw values log-normal distribution median 12.04 log-scale SD 0.8, clipped published range. Steroid use correlation weight. 85% steroid-use prevalence sampled independently weight BGENE21; paper describe joint distributions. Time-varying weight. Body weight treated time-fixed baseline covariate simulation. paper discuss intra-subject weight trajectories 52-week treatment period. Inter-occasion variability (IOV) V1. published final model included 26% CV IOV V1 first seven infusions . Occasion-specific random effects outside scope typical single-eta subject-level library model, IOV implemented. consequence individual peri-dose fluctuations simulation somewhat tighter observed data; steady-state exposure summaries (Cmax,ss, AUC(tau), Ctrough,ss) across full cohort materially affected. Infusion duration. Zheng 2016 specifies 30-60 min IV infusion; use 30 min. choice numerically immaterial given 28-day dosing interval ~24-day half-life. Dose covariate V1. source uses continuous (Dose/600)^0.06 covariate V1 driven subject’s assigned fixed dose level. preserve parameterisation exactly; subject’s DOSE covariate equals regimen dose (200, 600, 1200 mg).

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Structural model: 2-compartment IV input central, first-order elimination central, explicit depot absorption compartment. IIV: diagonal matrix CL V1; BSV Q V2. Covariates: WT, BGENE21, STEROID_BL CL; WT, DOSE, STEROID_BL V1. Collectively accounted < 10% PK variability published final model. Typical terminal half-life predicted structural parameters typical 64.3 kg patient BGENE21 = 12.04, DOSE = 600 mg, STEROID_BL = 0 approximately 24 days (dominated central-compartment elimination rate CL/V1 = 0.184/2.82 = 0.0652 day^-1 plus small peripheral redistribution term), consistent Results narrative.

title: Zheng_2016_sifalimumab
text: Zheng B, Yu X-Q, Greth W, Robbie GJ. Population pharmacokinetic analysis sifalimumab clinical phase IIb trial systemic lupus erythematosus patients. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2016;81(5):918-928. doi:10.1111/bcp.12864

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Citation: Zhu R, Zheng Y, Dirks NL, et al. Model-based clinical pharmacology profiling exposure-response relationships efficacy biomarker lebrikizumab patients moderate--severe asthma. Pulmonary Pharmacology & Therapeutics. 2017;46:88-98. doi:10.1016/j.pupt.2017.08.010 Description: Lebrikizumab population PK model (Zhu 2017): two-compartment model first-order absorption SC dosing adults moderate--severe asthma. Article: Pulmonary Pharmacology & Therapeutics. 2017;46:88-98

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: published analysis pooled 2148 subjects (21,917 PK observations) across six studies: two Phase studies healthy volunteers (n = 114), one Phase II asthma study, one Phase II atopic-dermatitis study, one Phase II idiopathic-pulmonary-fibrosis study, Phase III MILLY program moderate--severe asthma. Adults (age 18-75 years, median 48 years); body weight ranged 40-165 kg median near 77 kg; approximately 58% female. Race distribution majority White Black, Asian, “” categories separately encoded (distinct CL effect final covariate model). SC doses ranged 37.5 250 mg; IV data Phase studies also included. Three formulations evaluated: reference CHO formulation used late development, early-development NS0 formulation, interim “CHO Phase 2” formulation; indicator covariates FORM_NS0 FORM_CHO_PHASE2 zero reference formulation. information available programmatically via readModelDb("Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab")$population.

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Every structural parameter, covariate effect, IIV element, residual-error term taken directly Zhu 2017 Table 3. Reference values WT = 70 kg AGE = 40 years (Table 3 footnote). previous release model file stored six IIV entries sqrt(variance) rather variances/covariances; current version encodes raw variance-covariance matrix expected ~ c(...) form ini().

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Original observed data publicly available. cohort approximates Zhu 2017 Table 1: body weight drawn truncated normal centered reported median (77 kg, SD ~17 kg) limits 40 165 kg; age uniform 18-75 median ~48; SEXF ~ 58% female; race distribution White-majority Black, Asian, minorities; ADA-negative reference CHO formulation (FORM_NS0 = FORM_CHO_PHASE2 = 0). simulated regimen 125 mg SC every 4 weeks (Q4W), standard regimen studied MILLY program typical clinical dose.
code: set.seed(20260418) n_subj <- 400  race_levels <- c("white", "black", "asian", "other") race_probs <- c(0.75, 0.10, 0.10, 0.05)  cohort <- tibble::tibble(   id = seq_len(n_subj),   WT = pmin(pmax(rnorm(n_subj, mean = 77, sd = 17), 40), 165),   AGE = runif(n_subj, 18, 75),   SEXF = as.integer(runif(n_subj) < 0.58),   race = sample(race_levels, n_subj, replace = TRUE, prob = race_probs),   ADA_POS = 0L,   FORM_NS0 = 0L,   FORM_CHO_PHASE2 = 0L ) |>   dplyr::mutate(     RACE_BLACK = as.integer(race == "black"),     RACE_ASIAN = as.integer(race == "asian"),     RACE_OTHER = as.integer(race == "other")   )  # Q4W regimen: 6 SC doses (weeks 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20 = days 0, 28, 56, 84, 112, 140) # Observe to day 196 so the 5th and 6th dose cycles are at steady state. dose_days <- seq(0, 140, by = 28) obs_days <- sort(unique(c(   seq(0, 196, by = 2), # dense regular sampling   dose_days + 2, # early absorption peak window   dose_days + 0.25,   dose_days + 1 )))  ev_dose <- cohort |>   tidyr::crossing(time = dose_days) |>   dplyr::mutate(amt = 125, cmt = "depot", evid = 1L)  ev_obs <- cohort |>   tidyr::crossing(time = obs_days) |>   dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L)  events <- dplyr::bind_rows(ev_dose, ev_obs) |>   dplyr::arrange(id, time, dplyr::desc(evid)) |>   dplyr::select(id, time, amt, cmt, evid,     WT, AGE, SEXF, ADA_POS,     RACE_BLACK, RACE_ASIAN, RACE_OTHER,     FORM_NS0, FORM_CHO_PHASE2)

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
code: mod <- rxode2::rxode2(readModelDb("Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab")) sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod, events = events,   keep = c("WT", "AGE", "SEXF",     "RACE_BLACK", "RACE_ASIAN", "RACE_OTHER",     "ADA_POS", "FORM_NS0", "FORM_CHO_PHASE2"))

what: Cc-vs-time VPC over dose cycles 1 through 6
title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Population 5th / 50th / 95th percentile prediction bands 125 mg Q4W SC. Dose cycles 5 6 (days 112-168) represent approximate steady state given terminal half-life implied typical parameters (~3 weeks).
code: vpc <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(!is.na(Cc), time > 0) |>   dplyr::group_by(time) |>   dplyr::summarise(     Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(vpc, aes(time, Q50)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.2, fill = "#4682b4") +   geom_line(colour = "#4682b4", linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_vline(xintercept = dose_days, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, NA)) +   labs(     x = "Time (days)",     y = expression("Lebrikizumab Cc (" * mu * "g/mL)"),     title = "Simulated 5-50-95 percentile VPC, 125 mg Q4W SC (6 doses)",     caption = "Virtual cohort (N = 400); covariate distributions synthesized to approximate Zhu 2017 Table 1."   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: zoomed-view final dose cycle (days 140-168) isolate steady-state peak, trough, AUC_tau used .
code: ss_window <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time >= 140, time <= 168, !is.na(Cc))  ss_summary <- ss_window |>   dplyr::group_by(time) |>   dplyr::summarise(     Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),     Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),     Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),     .groups = "drop"   )  ggplot(ss_summary, aes(time - 140, Q50)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.2, fill = "#b4464b") +   geom_line(colour = "#b4464b", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     x = "Time after dose 6 (days)",     y = expression("Lebrikizumab Cc (" * mu * "g/mL)"),     title = "Steady-state dose cycle: 125 mg SC Q4W",     caption = "Median and 90% prediction interval over the 6th Q4W cycle."   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Non-compartmental analysis steady-state interval (days 140-168, dose 6). Compute Cmax, Ctrough (C end tau), AUC_tau per simulated subject, summarize across cohort.
code: nca_conc <- sim |>   dplyr::filter(time >= 140, time <= 168, !is.na(Cc)) |>   dplyr::mutate(time_nom = time - 140,     treatment = "125mg_Q4W_SS") |>   dplyr::select(id, time = time_nom, Cc, treatment)  nca_dose <- cohort |>   dplyr::mutate(time = 0, amt = 125, treatment = "125mg_Q4W_SS") |>   dplyr::select(id, time, amt, treatment)  conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(nca_conc, Cc ~ time | treatment + id) dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(nca_dose, amt ~ time | treatment + id)  intervals <- data.frame(   start   = 0,   end     = 28,   cmax    = TRUE,   cmin    = TRUE,   auclast = TRUE )  nca_res <- PKNCA::pk.nca(PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals)) #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■         78% |  ETA:  1s nca_tbl <- as.data.frame(nca_res$result) summary(nca_res) #>  start end    treatment   N    auclast        cmax        cmin #>      0  28 125mg_Q4W_SS 400 696 [39.9] 30.9 [37.9] 17.3 [46.2] #>  #> Caption: auclast, cmax, cmin: geometric mean and geometric coefficient of variation; N: number of subjects

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Zhu 2017 reports typical steady-state exposure 125 mg Q4W SC regimen Results / Discussion; typical subject (70 kg, age 40, white, male, ADA-negative, reference CHO formulation) typical-value prediction can computed IIV zeroed compared population medians . Typical-subject steady-state exposure: 125 mg SC Q4W (reference CHO, white, male, 70 kg, age 40, ADA-negative). Zhu 2017 reports 125 mg Q4W SC regimen delivers typical steady-state Cc low-ug/mL range (broadly consistent typical-subject Cmax_ss / Ctrough_ss shown ). Exact numeric comparison requires mean / median steady-state values reported Results text, vary depending whether paper summarized typical subject population mean; typical-value prediction serves primary structural check.
code: mod_typ <- rxode2::zeroRe(mod) typ_cohort <- tibble::tibble(   id = 1L, WT = 70, AGE = 40, SEXF = 0L,   RACE_BLACK = 0L, RACE_ASIAN = 0L, RACE_OTHER = 0L,   ADA_POS = 0L, FORM_NS0 = 0L, FORM_CHO_PHASE2 = 0L ) ev_typ <- dplyr::bind_rows(   typ_cohort |>     tidyr::crossing(time = dose_days) |>     dplyr::mutate(amt = 125, cmt = "depot", evid = 1L),   typ_cohort |>     tidyr::crossing(time = seq(0, 196, by = 0.25)) |>     dplyr::mutate(amt = 0, cmt = NA_character_, evid = 0L) ) |>   dplyr::arrange(id, time, dplyr::desc(evid))  sim_typ <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typ, events = ev_typ) #> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalka'  typ_ss <- sim_typ |>   as.data.frame() |>   dplyr::filter(time >= 140, time <= 168)  typ_metrics <- tibble::tibble(   Cmax_ss    = max(typ_ss$Cc, na.rm = TRUE),   Ctrough_ss = typ_ss$Cc[which.min(abs(typ_ss$time - 168))],   AUC_tau    = sum(diff(typ_ss$time) * (head(typ_ss$Cc, -1) + tail(typ_ss$Cc, -1)) / 2) ) knitr::kable(   typ_metrics, digits = 3,   caption = paste(     "Typical-subject steady-state exposure: 125 mg SC Q4W",     "(reference CHO, white, male, 70 kg, age 40, ADA-negative)."   ) )

title: Zhu_2017_lebrikizumab
text: Original data unavailable. virtual cohort synthesizes covariate distributions Zhu 2017 Table 1 summaries (adult range, median, sex fraction, race bands). Joint dependencies (e.g., age sex, race region) preserved. Formulation fixed reference CHO. FORM_NS0 = FORM_CHO_PHASE2 = 0 simulated subjects vignette characterizes commercial formulation; NS0 CHO-Phase-2 effects available model activated setting corresponding indicator 1. ADA status fixed negative. ADA_POS = 0 subjects (ADA prevalence Zhu 2017 cohorts low time-varying); CL effect 1.04 can activated setting ADA_POS = 1 individual. WT--CL exponent. Zhu 2017 Table 3 reports 1.00; unclear whether fixed 1.00 (classical allometry) estimated ~1.00. model file keeps estimated theta flags inline comment. AGE effect form. Encoded power form (AGE/40)^0.0241 per Table 3 footnote convention. small exponent makes effect size essentially negligible realistic ages, matches paper’s finding clinically meaningful AGE effect. IIV covariance fix. prior release stored sqrt(variance) etalcl + etalvc + etalka ~ c(...) block. corrected raw variance-covariance matrix Zhu 2017 Table 3, simulated -subject variability now consistent published model.

title: Creating a model library
text: Compartment parameter names lower case use snakeCase combined way. Compartment parameter names selected align used rxode2::linCmt() described vignette: vignette(“rxode2-model-types”, package = “rxode2”).

title: Creating a model library
text: Compartment naming follows compartment names linCmt() augmentation compartments: depot: extravascular dosing compartment (example, gut oral dosing subcutaneous space subcutaneous dosing) central: intravascular compartment used intravenous dosing typical pharmacokinetic (PK) model sampling drug peripheral1, peripheral2: first second peripheral compartments 2- 3-compartment PK models effect: compartment effect compartment models Therapeutic-area-specific models use consistent compartment parameter naming. adding new therapeutic area model library, please discuss naming first new GitHub issue.

title: Creating a model library
text: enable consistent cross-model compatibility, following conventions used unless strong reason exception: Pharmacokinetic concentrations central compartment named Cc. Cc used even using linCmt() model (case Cc <- linCmt() used residual error applied Cc parameter). Therapeutic-area-specific models use consistent compartment parameter naming. adding new therapeutic area model library, please discuss naming first new GitHub issue.

title: Creating a model library
text: PK models use following parameter naming conventions: ka: absorption rate cl: clearance q: intercompartmental clearance (central peripheral1 compartments) q2: second intercompartmental clearance (central peripheral2 compartments) vc: central volume distribution vp, vp2: first second peripheral compartment volumes micro-constants used, use following naming conventions: kel elimination rate (cl/vc) k12, k21, k13, k31: intercompartmental transit rates (q/vc, q/vp, q2/vc, q2/vp2, respectively)

title: Creating a model library
text: Parameters often estimated transformed scale. instance, natural logarithm transform often used parameters must positive, logit transform often used parameter must remain within specific range. Transformed parameters prefixed indicator transformation. Preferred transformation prefixes : l (lower case L): natural log transform logit: logit transform probit: probit transform transform similarly include full transform prefix Generally, transform natural logarithm, include full name prefix. example, natural logarithm-transformed ka lka logit-transformed emax logitemax.

text: Random effects estimates part distribution varying grouping factor. grouping factor often subject clinical trial. (NONMEM users, random effects often referred inter-individual variability.) Random effect parameters prefix (transformed) parameter name eta. example, random effect log-transformed clearance named etalcl.

title: Markov modeling
text: Start initial dataset previous state current state column. exercise, use hypothetical adverse event profile switching “none”, “mild”, “moderate” severities. make interpretability easier later, put states order, numeric. Putting levels order required, done, sorted alphabetically modeling may make sense (e.g. “none” coming “moderate”). dataset estimation requires one-hot encoded columns dataset. simply means need columns set 1 Markov state applies 0 . DV column required nlmixr2 work, ignored model.
code: dMarkov <-   rbind(     data.frame(       ID = 1,       TIME = 0:4,       previous = c("none", "mild", "moderate", "mild", "mild"),       current = c("mild", "moderate", "mild", "mild", "none")     ),     data.frame(       ID = 2,       TIME = 0:4,       previous = c("none", "moderate", "moderate", "none", "none"),       current = c("moderate", "moderate", "none", "none", "none")     ),     data.frame(       ID = 3,       TIME = 0:4,       previous = c("none", "mild", "mild", "mild", "none"),       current = c("none", "mild", "mild", "none", "mild")     )   ) stateLevels <- c("none", "mild", "moderate") dMarkov$previous <- factor(dMarkov$previous, levels = stateLevels) dMarkov$current <- factor(dMarkov$current, levels = stateLevels) dMarkov <- createMarkovModelDataset(dMarkov, colPrev = "previous", colCur = "current") knitr::knit_print(dMarkov) #>    ID TIME previous  current prevnone curnone prevmild curmild prevmoderate #> 1   1    0     none     mild     TRUE   FALSE    FALSE    TRUE        FALSE #> 2   1    1     mild moderate    FALSE   FALSE     TRUE   FALSE        FALSE #> 3   1    2 moderate     mild    FALSE   FALSE    FALSE    TRUE         TRUE #> 4   1    3        FALSE #> 12  3    1     mild     mild    FALSE   FALSE     TRUE    TRUE        FALSE #> 13  3    2     mild     mild    FALSE   FALSE     TRUE    TRUE        FALSE #> 14  3    3     mild     none    FALSE    TRUE     TRUE   FALSE        FALSE #> 15  3    4     none     mild     TRUE   FALSE    FALSE    TRUE        FALSE #>    curmoderate #> 1        FALSE #> 2         TRUE #> 3        FALSE #> 4        FALSE #> 5        FALSE #> 6         TRUE #> 7         TRUE #> 8        FALSE #> 9        FALSE #> 10       FALSE #> 11       FALSE #> 12       FALSE #> 13       FALSE #> 14       FALSE #> 15       FALSE dMarkov$DV <- 0

title: Markov modeling
text: automatically generate Markov model structure data, use createMarkovModel() function: point, model character string. Use advanced R methods (standard R functions) convert function nlmixr2. model can subsequently modified nlmixr2 model, example add drug effects, etc.
code: mod <- createMarkovModel(colPrev = dMarkov$previous, colCur = dMarkov$current) cat(mod) #> function() { #>   markovStates <- c(none = "none", mild = "mild", moderate = "moderate") #>   ini({ #>     logitnonetonone <- -0; label("Probability of transition from state none to none (logit probability)") #>     lognonetomild <- -1.099; label("Probability of transition from state none to mild (log-logit link difference from prior state)") #>     logitmildtonone <- -0.6931; label("Probability of transition from state mild to none (logit probability)") #>     logmildtomild <- -0.6931; label("Probability of transition from state mild to mild (log-logit link difference from prior state)") #>     logitmoderatetonone <- -0.6931; label("Probability of transition from state moderate to none (logit probability)") #>     logmoderatetomild <- -1.099; label("Probability of transition from state moderate to mild (log-logit link difference from prior state)") #>   }) #>   model({ #>     # Create the following one-hot encoded columns for previous and current Markov states (this can be done with `createMarkovModelDataset()`) #>     # For state none: prevnone, curnone #>     # For state mild: prevmild, curmild #>     # For state moderate: prevmoderate, curmoderate #>     # transition from state "none" to state "none" #>     linknonetonone <- logitnonetonone #>     cumprnonetonone <- expit(linknonetonone) #>     # transition from state "none" to state "mild" #>     linknonetomild <- linknonetonone + exp(lognonetomild) #>     cumprnonetomild <- expit(linknonetomild) #>     # Probability of each state transition #>     prnonetonone <- cumprnonetonone # Probability of transition from state none to none #>     prnonetomild <- cumprnonetomild - cumprnonetonone # Probability of transition from state none to mild #>     prnonetomoderate <- 1 - cumprnonetomild # Probability of transition from state none to moderate #>     # log-likelihood of any transition from state none #>     llnone <- prevnone*(curnone*log(prnonetonone) + curmild*log(prnonetomild) + curmoderate*log(prnonetomoderate)) #>     # transition from state "mild" to state "none" #>     linkmildtonone <- logitmildtonone #>     cumprmildtonone <- expit(linkmildtonone) #>     # transition from state "mild" to state "mild" #>     linkmildtomild <- linkmildtonone + exp(logmildtomild) #>     cumprmildtomild <- expit(linkmildtomild) #>     # Probability of each state transition #>     prmildtonone <- cumprmildtonone # Probability of transition from state mild to none #>     prmildtomild <- cumprmildtomild - cumprmildtonone # Probability of transition from state mild to mild #>     prmildtomoderate <- 1 - cumprmildtomild # Probability of transition from state mild to moderate #>     # log-likelihood of any transition from state mild #>     llmild <- prevmild*(curnone*log(prmildtonone) + curmild*log(prmildtomild) + curmoderate*log(prmildtomoderate)) #>     # transition from state "moderate" to state "none" #>     linkmoderatetonone <- logitmoderatetonone #>     cumprmoderatetonone <- expit(linkmoderatetonone) #>     # transition from state "moderate" to state "mild" #>     linkmoderatetomild <- linkmoderatetonone + exp(logmoderatetomild) #>     cumprmoderatetomild <- expit(linkmoderatetomild) #>     # Probability of each state transition #>     prmoderatetonone <- cumprmoderatetonone # Probability of transition from state moderate to none #>     prmoderatetomild <- cumprmoderatetomild - cumprmoderatetonone # Probability of transition from state moderate to mild #>     prmoderatetomoderate <- 1 - cumprmoderatetomild # Probability of transition from state moderate to moderate #>     # log-likelihood of any transition from state moderate #>     llmoderate <- prevmoderate*(curnone*log(prmoderatetonone) + curmild*log(prmoderatetomild) + curmoderate*log(prmoderatetomoderate)) #>     # Overall Markov model log-likelihood #>     llMarkov <- llnone + llmild + llmoderate #>     ll(err) ~ llMarkov #>   }) #> } modFun <- eval(str2lang(mod))

title: Markov modeling
